title: 20_01.dvi
journal: IPSJ Transactions on System LSI Design Methodology Vol.11 2–15 (Feb. 2018)
abstract: In advanced technology nodes, transistors and interconnects with shrinking physical dimensions suffer large process variations during manufacturing and are prone to reliability issues. These underlying changes require an overhaul of the design methodologies for digital circuits. In this paper, we provide an overview of techniques introduced recently to analyze the effect of uncertainty in manufacturing and reliability issues of devices due to the diminishing feature size. These techniques range from variation/aging modeling to circuit-level analysis. In addition, active techniques to counter these effects, such as clock skew tuning and voltage tuning are also covered in this paper.

Fig. 2 Setup time (tsu), hold time (th) and clock-to-q delay (dcq) of a flipflop.
Fig. 3 max and sum operations. a1–a5 are arrival times and d1–d4 represent delays of logic gates and interconnects.
Fig. 4 Variation Classification [3].
Fig. 5 Quadtree Correlation Model [8], [9].
Fig. 6 Uniform Grid Correlation Model [10].
Fig. 8 Grid partition of the chip in hierarchical statistical timing analysis.
Fig. 7 Timing paths in timing model extraction.
Fig. 9 Problem of timing analysis for sequential circuits considering dynamic power supply noise.
Fig. 10 An example of structural correlation in slack computation.
Fig. 11 Partial structure of general sequential circuit.
Fig. 12 SSTA accuracy and run time versus # of random variables associated with clock drivers.

Fig. 14 Delay curves of a flip-flop. (a) Curves of setup/hold slack combinations with respect to different constant clock-to-q delays. Setup and hold slacks are the time differences between the signal switches and the clock edge. (b) Characterization point of setup time and hold time in traditional STA.
Fig. 13 Statistical noise modeling for taking into consideration clock skew and jitter.
Fig. 15 Aging graph pruning [53] of the ISCAS85 circuit c17 depicted in (a). Timing graph of this circuit and the reduced graph are shown in (b) and (c), respectively.
Fig. 16 Post-silicon delay tunable buffer in Ref. [71].
Fig. 17 Pre-ordered compensation via body biasing.
Fig. 18 Error prediction based adaptive voltage scaling.
Fig. 19 Margins of circuits with and without adaptive speed control in chip lifetime.
Fig. 20 Margins of circuits with and without adaptive speed control in chip lifetime.
Fig. 21 Overview of stochastic error rate estimation.
Fig. 22 MTTF of CPI circuits.
